data.title: NetChoice urges DOGE to open door for more competitive software, cloud contracts: report
data.description:
Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL) and Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) might be eligible to acquire a larger share of federal contracts, and the new Department of Government Efficiency could make that goal more feasible, according to a report by Bloomberg.

Elon Musk's DOGE has apparently identified software spending as a possible source of excessive spending by the federal government. Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and its ubiquitous productivity software might be in the crosshairs, as Google aims to provide some of its own software to government agencies, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter. Meanwhile, Amazon Web Services, Google and Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) could also play a larger role in cloud computing solutions at the federal level.  

NetChoice, a technology trade group that promotes consumer choice, limited government and competition, sent a letter to DOGE last month. It expressed concerns over the federal government's technology procurement practices.

"We share the belief that billions can be saved through tried-and-true practices that are familiar to anyone in the private sector, especially promoting competition and efficiency in the government's $300 billion software estate," the letter said.

"The U.S. federal government is the world's largest IT purchaser but does not use that power to hold major vendors like Microsoft, which has an 85% monopoly on productivity software, accountable for abysmal cybersecurity performance," it said.

"The federal technology procurement process should result in the best-value products and services for both government employees and taxpayers," it continued. "Yet, time and time again, unelected federal bureaucrats' overemphasis on convenience and incumbency has resulted in agencies choosing the path of least resistance rather than selecting the best or most cost-effective solution."

NetChoice said that government programs must either use Microsoft's Azure Cloud to host the software or pay a substantial markup. They said offering other cloud service providers such as Google, AWS and Oracle could improve security. 

It's "concerning but unsurprising to see certain industry players trying to manipulate decision makers, through shadowy front groups, rather than competing transparently on price and quality," Microsoft spokesperson, Alex Huarek, said in a statement, according to Bloomberg.

Amazon declined to comment on the report. However, a response filed last year with the U.K.'s Competition and Markets Authority demonstrates Amazon's stance on fair software licensing.

"Competition authorities have long recognized that Microsoft has a very substantial degree of market power in the supply of productivity software," AWS wrote. 

A study conducted by Omdia found that Microsoft's share of productivity software in the public sector is about 90%, and more than seven times the share of the next largest competitor in the U.S.

"AWS customers expect to be able to use Microsoft's productivity software on these cloud-based desktop services, just as they would on a local computer ... However, as a result of the licensing restrictions, customers are either unable to use Windows software or are severely limited in the instances in which they can use Windows software for virtual desktop services hosted on third-party (AWS) infrastructure," AWS noted

"The inability of customers to run Microsoft software on services of Listed Providers, or the significant additional expense customers must incur to do so, prevents customers from running 'must-have' Microsoft software on a cloud services provider of their choice and is thereby undermining competition," they noted.

data.title: Trade war puts billions of dollars of IPO, M&A deals on ice - report
data.description:
Billions of dollars of acquisitions and initial public offerings were put on hold after President Donald Trump's massive tariff gambit shocked financial markets, according to media reports.

StubHub Holdings (STUB), Klarna Bank (KLAR), trading platform eToro (ETOR) MNTN (MNTN), and insurer Ategrity (ASIC) have all delayed planned public listings, Bloomberg News reported, citing people familiar with the matter. Other media outlets reported that Chime and Hinge Health (HNGE) are delaying public debuts.

In Friday trading, two days after Trump announced his sweeping plan to impose tariffs on goods from most countries, the Nasdaq fell into bear territory, and the Dow swooned into a correction. China struck back on Friday, announcing 34% tariffs on U.S. goods.

An executive in the IPO industry told Bloomberg that they expect all listings to be delayed for at least the next two weeks.

French buildings materials company Saint-Gobain (OTCPK:CODGF) (OTCPK:CODYY)  is holding off on the sale of its auto glass unit, which may have fetched as much as €2.5B ($2.8B). KKR (KKR), meanwhile, reportedly exited a group that's discussing the acquisition of Gerresheimer AG (OTCPK:GRRMF).

And debt being sold in leveraged finance markets to back deals is also hitting snags, including financing for HIG Capital's purchase of Converge Technology (OTCQX:CTSDF) (CTS:CA) and ABC Technologies' acquisition of TI Fluid Systems, Bloomberg said.

data.title: Wintrust Financial declares $0.50 dividend
data.description:
• Wintrust Financial (NASDAQ:WTFC) declares $0.50/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 2.08%
  • Payable May 22; for shareholders of record May 8; ex-div May 8.
  • See WTFC Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Meta working on $1B data center project in Wisconsin: report
data.description:
• Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META) is working on creating a data center project in Wisconsin to the tune of nearly $1B, Bloomberg reported.
  • Wisconsin signed an incentive deal with an unnamed company in February that would see an expected investment of $837M over several years, the news outlet added, citing documents. A person familiar with the matter told the news outlet that the company is Meta Platforms.
  • Meta shares closed down 5% on Friday, in-line with the broader market sell off.
  • Meta did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Dow, LyondellBasell cut at JPM as U.S. exports more polyethylene to China than any other country
data.description:
Dow (NYSE:DOW) and LyondellBasell (NYSE:LYB) -9.7% and -7.6%, respectively, in Friday's trading as J.P. Morgan downgrades the petrochem companies to Hold from Buy with respective $31 and $60 price targets, lowered from $40 and $80, saying "petrochemical demand is faltering, raw material costs are rising, trade risk is increasing, [and] global economic strength is weakening."

Dow (NYSE:DOW) is "in the most difficult position of the petrochemical companies," JPM's Jeffrey Zekauskas says, as weak demand in China and Europe, global capacity additions in each of the company's three business segments, higher interest rates, and a slow global  economy have eroded its EBITDA.

Dow (NYSE:DOW) offers an 8.9% dividend yield, a cyclically low earnings profile, and leverage to lower interest rates, but its value is sensitive to changes in global economic activity, and recessionary risk is rising, meaning the company's EBITDA along with its share price can fall sharply under recessionary conditions, the analyst writes, adding that each $10/bbl change in the Brent crude price probably impacts the company's annual EBITDA by ~$1B.

Zekauskas also is concerned that the market may view Dow's (DOW) hefty dividend yield as unsustainable given the volatility and uncertainty in quarterly EBITDA.

LyondellBasell (NYSE:LYB) is "a large, efficient commodity petrochemical company," Zekauskas says, but it is subject to many of the same stresses as Dow (DOW) in the context of declining market sentiment, rising recessionary risk and greater economic uncertainty.

Like Dow (DOW), LyondellBasell's (LYB) EBITDA can fall sharply under recessionary conditions in part due to the decline in oil prices, the analyst says, estimating each $10/bbl change in the Brent price probably affects the company's annual EBITDA by ~$400M.

data.title: Enterprise Bancorp shareholders approve merger into Independent Bank
data.description:
• Bank holding company Enterprise Bancorp (NASDAQ:EBTC) said on Friday that its shareholders have approved the proposed merger into Independent Bank (NASDAQ:INDB).
  • The special meeting of shareholders was held virtually on April 3.
  • The two companies signed a definitive merger agreement in December last year, with the cash and stock transaction involving a consideration valued at ~$562M, or $45.06 per share.

data.title: IGM Financial reports marginally lower AUM and advisement in March
data.description:
• Canada-based IGM Financial (TSX:IGM:CA) announced on Friday that its March 2025 AUM and advisement stood at $275.0B.
  • The figure compares with $278.2B in February, and $252.2B in March 31, 2024.
  • The asset manager reported net inflows of $3.3B for March 2025.

data.title: Ams outlines growth strategy with new Rhode Island and Mexico facilities
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: American Shared Hospital Services (AMS) Q4 2024

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Ray Stachowiak announced the appointment of Gary Delanois as the new CEO. He emphasized the company's strategic transition towards patient-centric services and highlighted their strong revenue growth driven by acquisitions and new facility openings.
  • Revenue for Q4 2024 increased 59% year-over-year, supported by the Rhode Island acquisition and the opening of a new radiation therapy treatment facility in Puebla, Mexico. Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter rose 29% compared to the same period in 2023.
  • CEO Gary Delanois detailed strategic investments in Rhode Island, including equipment upgrades and staffing optimization. He also highlighted the operational improvements achieved through the partnership with Brown University Health System for radiation oncologist staffing.
  • Delanois outlined the company's expansion into international markets with facilities in Peru, Ecuador, and new centers in Mexico, including a joint venture in Guadalajara.
  • CFO Scott Frech reported fiscal year 2024 revenue of $28.34 million, a 33% increase from 2023, with significant growth in the direct patient services segment. The company experienced a decline in the equipment leasing segment due to contract expirations and reduced Gamma Knife volumes.

Outlook

  • Management expects continued growth from the Rhode Island centers, supported by strategic partnerships and operational enhancements.
  • The company anticipates stronger international growth from increased treatment capabilities in Ecuador, expanded volumes in Peru, and operations from the new centers in Puebla and Guadalajara, Mexico.
  • Future growth opportunities include the development of a fourth radiation therapy center and a proton beam radiation therapy center in Rhode Island, both of which received Certificate of Need approvals.

Financial Results

  • Q4 2024 revenue was reported at $9.1 million, an increase of 59% compared to Q4 2023, driven by the Rhode Island acquisition and Puebla facility.
  • The direct patient services segment showed a 420% increase in revenue for Q4 2024, contrasting with a decline in the equipment leasing segment.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 2024 was $3.5 million, up from $2.7 million in Q4 2023.
  • Net loss for Q4 2024 was $1.3 million, compared to a net income of $415,000 in Q4 2023. The loss was influenced by increased reserves for impaired assets and removal costs.

Q&A

  • Analyst from Zacks inquired about benefits from the Rhode Island expansion and potential synergies. Stachowiak highlighted collaborative relationships with major healthcare providers in the state and strategic operational efficiencies.
  • An Eastwood Partners analyst questioned the impact of outdated equipment on referrals and the strategy for Gamma Knife performance. Stachowiak explained that equipment upgrades have restored referral volumes and outlined efforts to enhance community awareness of Gamma Knife centers.
  • A private investor expressed concerns about stock performance and transparency. Stachowiak emphasized the company's long-term strategic goals, geographic and product diversification, and the transition to patient services.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns over operational challenges and stock performance but acknowledged the growth potential of recent acquisitions and expansions.
  • Management maintained a confident tone regarding strategic initiatives and long-term growth, but demonstrated defensiveness when addressing current financial setbacks.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone reflected stronger confidence in the Rhode Island and international expansions but remained cautious about near-term challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4 revenue growth of 59% exceeded Q3's 36% year-over-year growth rate, driven by new facilities and acquisitions.
  • Adjusted EBITDA showed improvement from $1.37 million in Q3 to $3.5 million in Q4.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to a more confident outlook on strategic initiatives in Q4, particularly regarding Rhode Island and international operations.
  • Analysts' focus remained consistent on challenges with Gamma Knife volumes and operational efficiencies.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified challenges with integrating acquired facilities, including the need for equipment upgrades and staffing transitions.
  • Analysts raised concerns about Gamma Knife volume declines and the financial impact of contract expirations.
  • Management detailed mitigation strategies, including partnerships with healthcare providers, community awareness campaigns, and operational improvements.

Final Takeaway

American Shared Hospital Services emphasized its strategic pivot towards patient-centric services, supported by significant growth in direct patient care and international expansion. The new facilities in Rhode Island and Mexico, alongside operational upgrades, are expected to drive future performance. Despite current challenges in the leasing segment and Gamma Knife volumes, management remains confident in its long-term growth prospects and strategic direction.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Healthcare firms, Hinge, Medline are reportedly delaying IPOs amid market rout
data.description:
Digital physical therapy startup Hinge Health (HNGE) and medical supply firm Medline Industries are reportedly delaying their IPO plans in response to the market selloff driven by President Donald Trump’s sweeping tariff measures.

According to Business Insider, Hinge Health (HNGE), which filed for its long-awaited New York IPO last month, is looking to delay its offering as tariff concerns weigh on the U.S. markets.

The San Francisco, California-based firm had previously planned to launch its IPO in April, CNBC reported last month, citing a person familiar with the matter. Despite the delay, the company intends to stick to its spring IPO timeline,  Business Insider reported on Friday.

One of the largest manufacturers and distributors of medical supplies, Medline, has delayed its IPO plans indefinitely, The Financial Times reported on Friday. Medline, backed by private equity firms Blackstone (BX) and Carlyle (CG), was expected to announce its IPO filing earlier this week for a valuation of nearly $50B.

Additionally, Swedish fintech firm Klarna (KLAR) and ticketing marketplace StubHub (STUB) have reportedly delayed their much-awaited IPOs amid the market selloff.

data.title: Associated Capital updates on Q1 book value
data.description:
• Associated Capital Group (NYSE:AC) announced on Friday a preliminary range for its Q1 book value of $42.42-$42.62 per share.
  • The book value as of December 31, 2024, stood at $42.14.
  • The investment advisory services provider's shares were trading 9.32% down Friday late afternoon at $32.20.
  • The company is set to report its financial results in May.
